items.q: What is the key structural difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells?
items.correct: Eukaryotes have a membrane-bound nucleus and membrane-bound organelles; prokaryotes do not.
items.misconception: Prokaryotes have no DNA — that's why they have no nucleus.
items.random_wrong: Prokaryotes are always smaller than 1 micrometre and have no cytoplasm.
items.why: Students see 'no nucleus' and think 'no DNA', missing that prokaryotes still have DNA — it's just free in the cytoplasm.

items.q: What is the main role of mitochondria in a cell?
items.correct: To carry out aerobic respiration, producing ATP that cells use as an energy currency.
items.misconception: Mitochondria produce energy by creating it from nothing.
items.random_wrong: Mitochondria store sunlight and release it for photosynthesis.
items.why: The phrase 'powerhouse of the cell' makes students think energy is created by mitochondria, missing that they transfer energy from glucose to ATP.

items.q: In osmosis, which direction does water move?
items.correct: From a region of higher water potential (less concentrated solution) to lower water potential.
items.misconception: Water moves from high solute concentration to low solute concentration.
items.random_wrong: Water moves towards the sugar because sugar attracts water molecules chemically.
items.why: Students get confused between solute concentration and water potential; they flip the direction because 'high concentration' feels like it should attract.

items.q: What distinguishes active transport from facilitated diffusion?
items.correct: Active transport requires energy (ATP) and can move substances against the concentration gradient.
items.misconception: Both move substances down a concentration gradient, but active transport is faster.
items.random_wrong: Active transport only happens in animals and facilitated diffusion only in plants.
items.why: Students don't grasp that active transport's key feature is going against the gradient, not just 'using energy'.

items.q: What happens to an enzyme when it is denatured?
items.correct: Its tertiary structure (3D shape) changes, particularly the active site, so it can no longer bind substrate effectively.
items.misconception: The enzyme has died because enzymes are alive.
items.random_wrong: The enzyme has been completely broken into amino acids.
items.why: Students think of enzymes as alive (probably because they are produced by living things), so they describe denaturation as death rather than shape change.

items.q: Why are enzymes specific to certain substrates?
items.correct: Only substrates with complementary shape and chemistry to the active site can bind effectively.
items.misconception: Each enzyme can react with any substrate, but works fastest with one type.
items.random_wrong: Enzymes choose their substrate based on which one is most common in the cell.
items.why: Students underestimate how strict the lock-and-key/induced-fit relationship is; they think specificity is a preference rather than a structural requirement.

items.q: What is cellular respiration?
items.correct: A series of chemical reactions in cells that break down glucose to release energy as ATP.
items.misconception: Respiration means breathing — inhaling oxygen and exhaling carbon dioxide.
items.random_wrong: Respiration is the process by which plants make their own food using sunlight.
items.why: Everyday English equates 'respiration' with breathing; students rarely separate gas exchange from cellular metabolism.

items.q: Do plants respire?
items.correct: Yes — plants respire all the time to release energy; they also photosynthesise when there is light.
items.misconception: Plants only photosynthesise and do not respire; respiration is for animals only.
items.random_wrong: Plants only respire at night and only photosynthesise during the day; the processes never overlap.
items.why: Because photosynthesis is taught as the plant equivalent of respiration, students think it replaces respiration in plants entirely.

items.q: Where do plants get most of their biomass from?
items.correct: From carbon dioxide in the air — the carbon in plant tissue comes mostly from CO2 fixed during photosynthesis.
items.misconception: Plants get most of their mass from the soil through their roots.
items.random_wrong: Plants gain most of their mass directly from sunlight, which has weight.
items.why: Roots in soil look like the source of food/mass, but actually most plant dry mass is carbon from atmospheric CO2.

items.q: What does a gene code for?
items.correct: Typically a polypeptide (protein) — through transcription to mRNA and translation by ribosomes.
items.misconception: A gene codes for a specific physical feature like eye colour directly.
items.random_wrong: Each gene codes for an organ or body part as a whole.
items.why: Students learn 'gene for eye colour' shorthand, then think genes directly produce traits rather than producing proteins that contribute to traits.

items.q: What does it mean for an allele to be 'dominant'?
items.correct: It is expressed in the phenotype when at least one copy is present (in a heterozygote).
items.misconception: A dominant allele is the most common allele in a population.
items.random_wrong: A dominant allele physically destroys the recessive allele in the genome.
items.why: The everyday meaning of 'dominant' (most prevalent, strongest) misleads students into thinking it relates to frequency or power.

items.q: Why do mutations occur?
items.correct: They are random changes in DNA — caused by errors in replication or by mutagens — not directed by need.
items.misconception: Mutations occur because organisms need to adapt to their environment.
items.random_wrong: Mutations only happen in organisms that have been damaged by external chemicals.
items.why: Students confuse mutation with adaptation and think evolution involves organisms 'trying' to change — a Lamarckian rather than Darwinian view.

items.q: How does a population of organisms become better adapted to its environment over time?
items.correct: Individuals with advantageous heritable variations survive and reproduce more; their alleles increase in frequency over generations.
items.misconception: Individual organisms change during their lifetime in response to their environment and pass the changes on to offspring.
items.random_wrong: Organisms decide what features they need and grow them by force of will.
items.why: The Lamarckian 'inheritance of acquired characteristics' is intuitive — students think exercise-built muscles or learned skills get passed to children.

items.q: Did humans evolve from chimpanzees?
items.correct: No — humans and chimpanzees share a common ancestor; both have evolved from it.
items.misconception: Humans evolved directly from chimpanzees, which is why chimps still exist as a less-evolved version.
items.random_wrong: Humans evolved from monkeys but chimps evolved from gorillas.
items.why: The phrase 'descended from apes' and linear evolution diagrams encourage 'A turned into B' thinking, missing the branching tree model.

items.q: Why are food chains usually limited to about 4 or 5 trophic levels?
items.correct: Only ~10 percent of energy is transferred between trophic levels; eventually too little energy remains to support another level.
items.misconception: There aren't enough types of animals to fill more trophic levels.
items.random_wrong: Larger animals always eat the next ones up, and there aren't enough giant animals.
items.why: Students don't grasp the energy-loss aspect of food chains; they think the limit is biological diversity rather than thermodynamic efficiency.

items.q: How do decomposers return carbon to the atmosphere?
items.correct: They respire — breaking down dead organic matter and releasing CO2.
items.misconception: Decomposers release carbon by physically breaking matter into smaller pieces.
items.random_wrong: Decomposers turn dead matter into oxygen and water, with no carbon involved.
items.why: Students see decomposition as a physical breakdown rather than a metabolic respiration process happening in microbes and fungi.

items.q: What does negative feedback do in homeostasis?
items.correct: It reverses a change in a controlled variable, bringing it back towards the set point.
items.misconception: Negative feedback makes a process get worse or amplifies the change.
items.random_wrong: Negative feedback stops the body from responding to any internal change.
items.why: The word 'negative' has bad connotations, so students think it makes things worse, missing that it counteracts deviations.

items.q: How are signals transmitted across a synapse between two neurones?
items.correct: Via neurotransmitter chemicals released from the presynaptic neurone, which bind receptors on the postsynaptic neurone.
items.misconception: The electrical signal jumps directly from one neurone to the next across the synaptic gap.
items.random_wrong: Neurones touch each other at synapses and pass current through direct contact.
items.why: Students think of nerves as 'wires' carrying electricity straight through, missing the chemical step at each synapse.

items.q: Why is a reflex action faster than a conscious response?
items.correct: The signal does not need to be processed by the brain — it travels through a simpler reflex arc via the spinal cord.
items.misconception: The brain decides reflexes faster than other actions because it skips thinking.
items.random_wrong: Reflexes are faster because they use specially fast neurones not used elsewhere.
items.why: Students assume the brain is always in charge; they don't realise reflexes specifically bypass the brain to save time.

items.q: How do vaccines protect you from a disease?
items.correct: They expose your immune system to a harmless form of the pathogen so memory cells form, allowing rapid response if exposed later.
items.misconception: Vaccines kill the pathogen if it ever enters the body.
items.random_wrong: Vaccines provide antibodies for life by adding them directly to the blood.
items.why: Students think vaccines actively fight pathogens themselves, rather than priming the immune system to fight.

items.q: What makes antibodies effective against pathogens?
items.correct: They have a specific shape complementary to antigens on a particular pathogen, marking it for destruction or neutralising it.
items.misconception: Antibodies are general-purpose proteins that work against any pathogen.
items.random_wrong: Antibodies are tiny knives that physically cut pathogens apart.
items.why: Students don't grasp antibody specificity, often picturing them as generic 'germ killers' rather than precision tools matching one antigen.

items.q: How do hormones reach their target organs?
items.correct: They are carried in the bloodstream and act on cells with specific receptors for that hormone.
items.misconception: Hormones are sent through nerves directly to specific organs.
items.random_wrong: Hormones are released directly into target organs by being injected by glands.
items.why: Students conflate nervous and endocrine signalling; they think every signal in the body travels through nerves.

items.q: What does insulin do to blood glucose?
items.correct: It lowers blood glucose by causing cells (especially liver and muscle) to take it up and store it as glycogen.
items.misconception: Insulin raises blood glucose to give the body energy.
items.random_wrong: Insulin destroys glucose in the bloodstream so it cannot be used.
items.why: Students associate sugar with energy and 'doing something with sugar' with raising energy, missing that insulin removes glucose from blood.

items.q: What drives water up the xylem in a tall tree?
items.correct: Transpiration pull — evaporation from leaves creates tension that pulls water up the continuous water column.
items.misconception: Roots actively pump water up to the leaves like a heart.
items.random_wrong: Capillary action alone is enough to lift water to the top of any tree.
items.why: Students assume there must be an active 'pump' system like circulation in animals; they miss that the evaporation at the top does the work.

items.q: What is transported in the phloem of a plant?
items.correct: Sugars (mainly sucrose) and other organic compounds, from sources (e.g. leaves) to sinks (e.g. roots, fruits).
items.misconception: Phloem transports water from roots to leaves.
items.random_wrong: Phloem transports oxygen to all parts of the plant.
items.why: Students mix up xylem (water) and phloem (sugar); they don't link the function with each tissue.

items.q: What is the key difference between mitosis and meiosis?
items.correct: Mitosis produces two genetically identical diploid cells; meiosis produces four genetically different haploid cells (gametes).
items.misconception: Mitosis and meiosis both produce gametes; they are basically the same.
items.random_wrong: Mitosis happens in plants and meiosis happens in animals.
items.why: The similar names lead to confusion; students mix up which process produces gametes vs body cells.

items.q: Where does transcription occur in a eukaryotic cell?
items.correct: In the nucleus — DNA is transcribed to mRNA, which then travels to the cytoplasm.
items.misconception: Transcription happens on ribosomes in the cytoplasm.
items.random_wrong: Transcription happens in mitochondria where ATP is produced.
items.why: Students mix transcription and translation — translation happens on ribosomes, transcription happens in the nucleus.

items.q: Why is interspecific competition between similar species important?
items.correct: Two species cannot occupy exactly the same niche indefinitely — competition often leads to displacement or niche divergence.
items.misconception: Competition only happens between predators and their prey.
items.random_wrong: Competition means physically fighting and is rare in nature.
items.why: Students associate 'competition' with literal fighting, missing the ecological meaning of competing for shared resources.

items.q: How does sweating help cool you down?
items.correct: Evaporation of sweat absorbs energy (latent heat) from the skin, lowering body temperature.
items.misconception: Sweat is cold and directly cools the skin on contact.
items.random_wrong: Sweating removes excess heat by carrying it out as liquid waste.
items.why: Students don't connect sweating to evaporation; they think the liquid itself is the cooling agent, not the phase change.

items.q: What is produced in anaerobic respiration in human muscle cells?
items.correct: Lactic acid (lactate) and a small amount of ATP — no CO2 or water in this pathway.
items.misconception: Anaerobic respiration produces ethanol and CO2 in humans.
items.random_wrong: Anaerobic respiration produces oxygen as a waste product.
items.why: Students confuse anaerobic respiration in yeast (alcohol + CO2) with the human form (lactic acid).

items.q: What is a stem cell?
items.correct: An undifferentiated cell capable of dividing to produce more stem cells or differentiating into specialised cell types.
items.misconception: Stem cells are taken only from embryos and cannot be found in adults.
items.random_wrong: Stem cells are simply cells that haven't grown big enough yet.
items.why: Media coverage of embryonic stem cells overshadows adult stem cells, so students think 'stem cell' = 'embryo cell'.

items.q: Do all arteries carry oxygenated blood?
items.correct: No — the pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood from the heart to the lungs.
items.misconception: All arteries carry oxygenated blood and all veins carry deoxygenated blood.
items.random_wrong: Arteries and veins both carry the same mixed blood through the body.
items.why: The simplification 'arteries = oxygenated, veins = deoxygenated' is taught early and not nuanced for the pulmonary circuit.

items.q: Why does the left ventricle have a thicker muscular wall than the right?
items.correct: It must pump blood at high pressure around the whole body; the right ventricle only pumps to the nearby lungs.
items.misconception: The left ventricle is thicker because it handles oxygenated blood, which is harder to pump.
items.random_wrong: The left ventricle is thicker because it is on the same side as the brain.
items.why: Students focus on blood type (oxygenated vs not) rather than the pressure needed to push blood around the systemic circuit.

items.q: Why are X-linked recessive conditions more common in males?
items.correct: Males have only one X chromosome, so a single recessive allele is expressed — there's no second X to mask it.
items.misconception: X-linked recessive conditions affect more males because the Y chromosome makes the disease worse.
items.random_wrong: X-linked conditions affect only males because females cannot inherit X-linked alleles.
items.why: Students don't grasp hemizygosity — they think the Y chromosome must be active in the inheritance, instead of absent for that gene.

items.q: What does 'semi-conservative' DNA replication mean?
items.correct: Each new DNA molecule contains one original (parental) strand and one newly synthesised strand.
items.misconception: One daughter molecule contains both original strands; the other contains both new strands.
items.random_wrong: DNA replication produces two identical fully new molecules with no original material.
items.why: Students confuse semi-conservative with conservative replication — they don't grasp that each daughter molecule is a hybrid of old and new strands, as Meselson-Stahl demonstrated.

items.q: Where does transcription happen in a eukaryotic cell, and what is the product?
items.correct: In the nucleus — DNA is copied into mRNA, which then travels to the cytoplasm for translation.
items.misconception: Transcription happens at ribosomes, where DNA is copied directly into protein.
items.random_wrong: Transcription happens in the mitochondria during respiration.
items.why: Students conflate transcription (DNA→mRNA, in the nucleus) with translation (mRNA→protein, at ribosomes); they think it's all one process at the ribosome.

items.q: What reads the mRNA codons during translation?
items.correct: tRNA molecules carrying specific amino acids — their anticodons pair with mRNA codons at the ribosome.
items.misconception: DNA directly reads the codons on mRNA and adds amino acids.
items.random_wrong: Ribosomes synthesise amino acids from scratch using the mRNA as a template.
items.why: Students miss the role of tRNA as the adaptor; they think mRNA is read by a direct chemical process rather than via tRNA matching.

items.q: What is the difference between a codon and an anticodon?
items.correct: A codon is a triplet of bases on mRNA; an anticodon is the complementary triplet on tRNA that pairs with it.
items.misconception: Codons are on DNA and anticodons are on mRNA.
items.random_wrong: Codons and anticodons are the same thing seen from two angles.
items.why: Students mix up where codons and anticodons sit; they think the codon is on the DNA strand because that's the original code.

items.q: After pre-mRNA splicing in eukaryotes, which sequences remain in the mature mRNA?
items.correct: Exons remain; introns are removed.
items.misconception: Introns remain because they encode the protein; exons are removed as junk.
items.random_wrong: Both introns and exons are removed and the cell uses just the cap and tail.
items.why: Students confuse the EX- (expressed) and IN- prefixes; the naming is the opposite of what intuition suggests.

items.q: What is the difference between a silent and a missense mutation?
items.correct: A silent mutation changes a base but the codon still codes for the same amino acid; a missense mutation changes the amino acid encoded.
items.misconception: A silent mutation has no effect because no base actually changes.
items.random_wrong: Missense and silent mutations always produce a non-functional protein.
items.why: Students think 'silent' means 'no change at the DNA level' rather than 'no change at the protein level' (due to genetic code degeneracy).

items.q: Why are frameshift mutations usually more severe than point substitutions?
items.correct: Insertions/deletions of a non-multiple of 3 shift the reading frame, changing every codon downstream of the mutation.
items.misconception: Frameshifts only change the codon at the site of the mutation, like a substitution.
items.random_wrong: Frameshift mutations are usually silent because cells can correct them.
items.why: Students see 'one base inserted' and think 'one amino acid changed', missing that the entire downstream reading frame shifts.

items.q: What are the three repeated stages of one PCR cycle?
items.correct: Denaturation (high temp, strands separate), annealing (lower temp, primers bind), extension (Taq polymerase synthesises new strands).
items.misconception: PCR has just one stage — heating the DNA until it copies itself.
items.random_wrong: PCR uses enzymes from bacteria to cut DNA into fragments, then sticks them back together.
items.why: Students don't grasp the three-temperature cycle; they think PCR is just 'heating DNA' rather than a precise three-step process repeated 30+ times.

items.q: What is the role of the guide RNA in CRISPR-Cas9?
items.correct: It directs Cas9 to the specific DNA sequence to cut by base-pairing with the target.
items.misconception: Guide RNA is the new sequence that gets inserted into the genome.
items.random_wrong: Guide RNA is the enzyme that cuts the DNA.
items.why: Students confuse the guide RNA (which targets) with Cas9 (which cuts) and with any donor DNA (which is inserted) — three distinct components.

items.q: What is the role of DNA ligase when inserting a gene into a plasmid?
items.correct: It forms phosphodiester bonds that join the gene to the cut plasmid, sealing the recombinant DNA.
items.misconception: DNA ligase cuts the plasmid open so the gene can be inserted.
items.random_wrong: DNA ligase reads the gene and translates it into protein.
items.why: Students confuse the cutting enzymes (restriction endonucleases) with the joining enzyme (ligase); both are needed but do opposite jobs.

items.q: What distinguishes the innate from the adaptive immune response?
items.correct: Innate is fast, non-specific, and has no memory; adaptive is slower initially, highly specific, and forms memory cells.
items.misconception: Innate immunity attacks viruses and adaptive attacks bacteria.
items.random_wrong: Innate immunity only works in children; adaptive only works in adults.
items.why: Students think the two systems target different pathogens, missing that they differ in speed, specificity, and memory — not target.

items.q: Why is the secondary immune response to a pathogen faster and stronger than the primary?
items.correct: Memory cells generated during the primary response can rapidly proliferate and produce large amounts of specific antibody on re-exposure.
items.misconception: The pathogen becomes weaker after the primary exposure, so the body has an easier job.
items.random_wrong: The body produces a different, more powerful antibody during the secondary response.
items.why: Students attribute the difference to the pathogen rather than to memory cells (B and T memory lymphocytes), missing the role of immunological memory.

items.q: What is the main role of B-cells compared to T-cells?
items.correct: B-cells produce antibodies (humoral response); T-cells coordinate the immune response and kill infected cells (cell-mediated response).
items.misconception: B-cells and T-cells both produce antibodies in equal amounts.
items.random_wrong: T-cells produce antibodies and B-cells kill infected cells.
items.why: Students mix up which cell type does what; the B/T naming gives no intuitive clue to function.

items.q: What makes the antigen-binding site of an antibody specific to one antigen?
items.correct: The variable region — its amino acid sequence (and thus shape) differs between antibodies, complementing a specific antigen.
items.misconception: All antibodies have the same binding site; specificity comes from where they go in the body.
items.random_wrong: Antibodies bind to any antigen they meet first.
items.why: Students miss the role of the variable region in determining specificity, thinking antibodies are interchangeable generic 'germ-killers'.

items.q: What is actually transferred to the body when you get a vaccine?
items.correct: Antigens (often from weakened, dead, or fragmented pathogens) that train the immune system to make memory cells.
items.misconception: Vaccines inject ready-made antibodies directly into the bloodstream.
items.random_wrong: Vaccines insert new genes into your DNA permanently.
items.why: Students confuse vaccines (which prompt your own immune response) with passive immunity (which delivers ready-made antibodies, e.g. antivenom).

items.q: During an action potential, do Na+ and K+ move through the same channels?
items.correct: No — voltage-gated Na+ channels open first (depolarisation, Na+ in), then voltage-gated K+ channels open (repolarisation, K+ out).
items.misconception: Na+ and K+ swap places through the same channel at the same time.
items.random_wrong: Only Na+ moves during an action potential; K+ stays still.
items.why: Students simplify ion movement as 'they swap' rather than recognising two separate channel types opening sequentially.

items.q: What does the 'all-or-nothing' principle mean for action potentials?
items.correct: Once threshold is reached, the action potential fires at full size — sub-threshold stimuli produce no action potential.
items.misconception: Stronger stimuli produce larger action potentials.
items.random_wrong: Action potentials get bigger as they travel along the axon.
items.why: Students think 'more stimulus = bigger response' (analogous to volume control), missing that stimulus strength is coded by FREQUENCY of action potentials, not amplitude.

items.q: What is the purpose of the refractory period after an action potential?
items.correct: It ensures action potentials travel in one direction only and limits the maximum firing frequency.
items.misconception: The refractory period exists because the cell runs out of energy and needs a rest.
items.random_wrong: The refractory period blocks all signals from reaching the neurone.
items.why: Students think of it as a 'rest break' rather than a functional feature ensuring unidirectional propagation.

items.q: How does myelination increase the speed of nerve impulse conduction?
items.correct: Action potentials jump between nodes of Ranvier (saltatory conduction), skipping the myelinated sections.
items.misconception: Myelin makes the axon thicker so the signal travels faster through more material.
items.random_wrong: Myelin contains additional ion channels that speed up depolarisation.
items.why: Students think 'more material = faster' rather than understanding that myelin INSULATES so the impulse can leap between gaps (saltatory).

items.q: Why is negative feedback the default mechanism in homeostasis?
items.correct: It opposes deviations from a set point, returning the variable toward its normal value — keeping internal conditions stable.
items.misconception: Negative feedback amplifies changes to make them more noticeable.
items.random_wrong: Negative feedback only operates when something goes wrong in the body.
items.why: The word 'negative' confuses students into thinking it amplifies/worsens, but in control theory 'negative' just means 'opposes the change'.

items.q: How do insulin and glucagon work together to regulate blood glucose?
items.correct: They are antagonistic — insulin lowers blood glucose (cells take up glucose, store as glycogen); glucagon raises it (glycogen broken back down to glucose).
items.misconception: Insulin and glucagon both lower blood glucose using different mechanisms.
items.random_wrong: Insulin works on muscles and glucagon works on the brain.
items.why: Students don't grasp 'antagonistic hormones' — they often think both hormones do similar jobs because both are about glucose.

items.q: How does ADH affect the collecting duct in the kidney?
items.correct: It increases the permeability of the collecting duct to water (by inserting aquaporins), so more water is reabsorbed and urine is more concentrated.
items.misconception: ADH actively pumps water out of urine into the blood.
items.random_wrong: ADH directly opens holes in the collecting duct that let urine escape.
items.why: Students think hormones must 'do' something dramatic; they miss that ADH just changes membrane permeability, allowing osmosis to do the work.

items.q: When you are too hot, what happens to the arterioles in your skin?
items.correct: They vasodilate — more blood flows close to the skin surface, releasing heat to the environment by radiation.
items.misconception: The blood vessels move up to the surface of the skin to release heat.
items.random_wrong: Capillaries split open to release blood and cool the body.
items.why: Diagrams show vessels appearing closer to the surface, leading students to think vessels physically move (rather than the same vessels dilating/constricting).

items.q: Which direction does water move in the xylem, and what drives it?
items.correct: Upwards from roots to leaves — driven mainly by transpiration pull (evaporation at leaves creating tension on the water column).
items.misconception: Water moves up the xylem because roots pump it actively using ATP.
items.random_wrong: Water moves both directions in the xylem depending on the time of day.
items.why: Students assume an active 'pump' is needed; they don't realise evaporation at the top, combined with water's cohesion, pulls the whole column up.

items.q: Why is the cohesion-tension theory critical for water transport in plants?
items.correct: Hydrogen bonds between water molecules keep the water column unbroken under tension, so transpiration can pull water up from the roots.
items.misconception: Cohesion-tension describes how water diffuses freely between cells.
items.random_wrong: Cohesion-tension explains how plants store water in the xylem like a reservoir.
items.why: Students miss the mechanical aspect — that water is under tension (negative pressure) and only hydrogen bonding keeps the column intact.

items.q: Can sucrose move in both directions in the phloem?
items.correct: Yes — translocation goes from any source (e.g. leaves) to any sink (e.g. roots, fruits), so direction varies seasonally and between phloem sieve tubes.
items.misconception: Phloem always carries sucrose downwards from leaves to roots.
items.random_wrong: Phloem carries water upwards and sucrose downwards in the same tube.
items.why: Students conflate phloem with xylem in terms of being unidirectional; they don't realise source-sink relationships can reverse (e.g. early spring, roots are sources).

items.q: How is sucrose loaded into the phloem at a source?
items.correct: By active transport (using ATP) via companion cells, against a concentration gradient.
items.misconception: Sucrose diffuses passively into the phloem because it is more concentrated outside.
items.random_wrong: Sucrose is pushed into the phloem by turgor pressure from leaf cells.
items.why: Students don't realise translocation requires energy at the loading step; they think 'flow' implies passive movement throughout.

items.q: What actually changes during natural selection in a population?
items.correct: The frequencies of alleles in the population's gene pool change across generations.
items.misconception: Individual organisms change their own genes to adapt.
items.random_wrong: The entire DNA sequence of every individual changes simultaneously.
items.why: Students think evolution happens within individuals (Lamarckian); they miss that natural selection operates on POPULATIONS via allele frequencies over generations.

items.q: Why is ATP described as an 'energy currency' rather than energy storage?
items.correct: ATP is constantly made and broken down — it transfers energy between reactions rather than storing large amounts for later.
items.misconception: Cells store large amounts of ATP for emergency use.
items.random_wrong: ATP is permanent and accumulates with age in cells.
items.why: Students treat ATP as a battery that fills up and gets used; in reality, cells make ATP just-in-time and have only seconds' worth at any moment.
